draft.introduction: Blue Cliff Record Case 2: Zhaozhou asks Nanquan about the Way. “Ordinary mind is the Way.” But if one aims for it, one misses it. The paradox of seeking what cannot be sought.

translation.text: Zhaozhou asked Nanquan: “What is the Way?” Nanquan said: “Ordinary mind is the Way.” Zhaozhou said: “Should I try to direct myself toward it?” Nanquan said: “If you try to direct yourself toward it, you betray your own practice.” Zhaozhou said: “If I do not try, how will I know it is the Way?” Nanquan said: “The Way does not belong to knowing or not knowing. Knowing is delusion; not knowing is blankness.”
translation.original_text: 趙州問南泉、如何是道、南泉云、平常心是道

translator_notes.note: The paradox—aiming at ordinary mind makes it extraordinary, while not aiming leaves one lost—is Zen’s central pedagogical trap. Nanquan negates both knowing and not-knowing, leaving Zhaozhou in a productive impasse that only non-conceptual realization can resolve.
